item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: the highlighting doesn't work on mobile ): had to wait til i got back to my computer to check. still confused tho

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I really like this SCP, the way you hid the messages was a nice touch and the Agent Weber bit got me confused but I think I understand it now that I thought about it again. Here is what I thing is going on for those who want another explanation of what it is, though I might be incorrect.

So from what I gather people who enter the room leave a "ghost" or "copy" of themselves in a mirror dimension that is linked to the foundations universe. It would appear Agent Weber's copy managed to escape this room in the mirror dimension. When people address a person affected by SCP-1383 they appear to be only able to acknowledge the copy of the person. So it would make sense that since Agent Weber's ghost escaped the room people would address him at the location the copy is at. I think that the copys can affect things in there mirror world and peoples views on things will change accordingly, such as a copy in the mirror world killing a person and people thinking that person is dead even though he is still alive and talk to that person.

If you have any other ideas or suggestions on this topic please do comment

item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded: The image that was used in this article could not be sourced, and thus is assumed to be non-compliant with the SCP wiki's license (CC BY-SA 3.0). As such, the image has been removed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I liked the idea, but I didn't get the rest of the story. After reading all the comments, I still don't know why I didn't get it. Maybe I missed the point, or maybe I missed some backstory. Either way, it's unfortunate such a good idea feels lost in whatever the cause of confusion is.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: To be honest, I actually like the large black boxing. It reminds me of classified documents in old Cold War movies and stuff.

item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded:
Timer Expired. Edits made as discussed below. Author may revert at any time.

The WikiWalk team is proposing edits to this page. All proposed edits will be in a collapsible below this post, and after 7 days if there are no serious objections to the edits, the feedback received will be taken into account as the edits are made. If you have questions not directly related to the article about this project, please pm a member of the WikiWalk Team

+ Show Block
- Hide Block

I was lucky enough to get a chance to speak with Silber regarding this article in the IRC, about his headcanon for it and suitable links, when he brought to my attention that CECE is a direct reference to the Wanderer's Library article CÉCE Designate 12-Pulaski-Maraschino. While the point of the wikiwalk is to expand the crosslinking between tales and articles posted here on the SCP wiki, I can see no real reason why a WL article directly referenced within an SCP should be exempt from getting attention. As such, I am recommending the following edits:

Changing this:

Reported conceptual written content includes various expletives and indecipherable or obscene symbols, as well as a crudely-drawn SCP Foundation logo and small notes in Spanish, English, and an unknown language, including "COMO ME SALGO DE ESTE JUEGO"1 and "CALL CÉCE DESIGNATE ██But one of the axes may be linear time.██".2

To this:

Reported conceptual written content includes various expletives and indecipherable or obscene symbols, as well as a crudely-drawn SCP Foundation logo and small notes in Spanish, English, and an unknown language, including "COMO ME SALGO DE ESTE JUEGO"3 and "CALL CÉCE DESIGNATE ██But one of the axes may be linear time.██".4

If anyone has other/better examples that could be used here please leave a comment saying so!

Footnotes
1. "How do I get out of this game?"
2. — — — —— —— —— —— —— —— —— —— —— —— —— —— —— —— —— —— —— —— —— —— —— —— —— —— —— —— —— —— —— —— —— —— —— —— —— —— —— —— —— —— —— —— —— —— —— —— —— —— —— —— —— —— —— —— —Which would mean just having to wait for competition to begin. — —— —— —— —— —— —— —— —— —— —— —— —— —— —— —— —— —— —— —— —— —— —— —— —— —— —— —— —— —— —— —— —— —— —— —— —— —— —— —— —— —— —— —— —— —— —— —— —— —— —— —— —— —Patience, dear Caliginator.— —— —— —— —— —— —— —— ——
3. "How do I get out of this game?"
4. — — — —— —— —— —— —— —— —— —— —— —— —— —— —— —— —— —— —— —— —— —— —— —— —— —— —— —— —— —— —— —— —— —— —— —— —— —— —— —— —— —— —— —— —— —— —— —— —— —— —— —— —— —— —— —— —Which would mean just having to wait for competition to begin. — —— —— —— —— —— —— —— —— —— —— —— —— —— —— —— —— —— —— —— —— —— —— —— —— —— —— —— —— —— —— —— —— —— —— —— —— —— —— —— —— —— —— —— —— —— —— —— —— —— —— —— —— —Patience, dear Caliginator.— —— —— —— —— —— —— —— ——

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Took the liberty to remove an extra "has" that wasn't supposed to be there.

Overall, I like the general idea behind this one but it's just a little too vague for my tastes and doesn't entirely sit right with me. But you quite clearly spent a lot of time on this and put a lot of thought into it so there must be something I'm missing.

Novote for now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Goddammit, this is cool.

a green-hued sphere of rock

We know it isn't round. We know it isn't green.

ayyyyyyyyyyyyyyy

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Belated upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Upvote due to the fact that it left me thinking:

"What the actual fuck did I just read?"

Very well done. Still not sure I understand it. But it's awesome.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
*reads article* Okay, that was… interesting. Not really getting much after the "people think you're still there" part, but I still like it for some reason. I wonder what the comments say.
*reads first few comments*
*goes back and highlights all the blackboxes*
…
OHGODTHATISCREEPYWHY!?!?
But seriously. I have no idea what this thing is or does besides that the asteroid/nonexistent moon of Saturn Themis seems to somehow be the key locking the concepts in the room and that "breaking" it can let them out and the dolphin-intelligence-level concept entity shaped like Weber was up to something. Or something. But only two other SCPs (1981 and 1425) have freaked me out like this, so that's worth an upvote for creepiness alone. Funny how out of all the stuff on this site, a Betamax tape, a self-help book and an empty hotel room are the only things that genuinely scared me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Normally I'm a sucker for these kinds of articles, with well-hidden hooks and interface-screws that draw you in. I really really want to like this one, but I just can't wrap my head around it.

+ Show Nova's Descent Into Madness
- Hide Nova's Descent Into Madness

Initially, I also missed the blackbox messages because, like others have mentioned, the first line of blackboxes has no hidden info. I realize after so many suggestions to put a secret in there have been made, and that there still isn't one, means that the author does not like the idea. I would like to offer a compromise. If you deliberately want people to check the first blackbox and see no message, and only start seeing them in the second line, then perhaps just include an elipsis in the first blackbox lines. Something like — — — … — — — —. Just something, ANYTHING to keep them highlighting further boxes.

Second, I find it hard to wrap my head around the general concept of the article. Maybe that's the intent and sorta meta with the play between "real" and "concept" going on. I get that the concept of a person gets "stuck" in the room, but I'm not entirely certain what goes on when other people view them beyond "Aren't you back in that room?". It seems like there are additional effects, but that is the precise point at which I got lost. Everything after "Aren't you in that room?" is Greek to me.

Then the thing started changing its effect and I just couldn't keep up. There was another SCP in there somewhere that was a dolphin? And the concept-Agent brought the Dolphin-SCP into the Room? Isn't the concept-Agent stuck in the room? How did he get out and get to the Dolphin? And how did a concept interact with real things in the first place? Why would the concept-Agent murder random people? That doesn't sound like he's trying to maintain normalcy like a Foundation Agent would. I started to think that the Concept-Agent was just a completely different entity that only appeared to look like the Agent. And what happened to the Dolphin? There's a Concept-Dolphin and a Real-Dolphin, but who took the Real-Dolphin out of the room?

I just… b'guh. Lost. I was almost going to downvote this, except I have the crazy suspicion that me not getting it is my fault, and there's something simple I'm just not seeing. No vote instead.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I've read this over twice and I still don't get it at all.

It took me between 7 and 9 viewings to completely get "Memento" though, so I hold out hope for this one, but as of now it's all just narrowly blowing past my head.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
My first reaction to seeing this was:

"Oh my god why didn't this person just use "Data Expunged" "

But after reading the discussion I understand the little "trick" now. Neat little trick, confusing concept that forced me to read this more times than I would like, downvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This SCP is one of the most confusing ones I've ever read. I believe that the basic gist of it is that our world exists in two dimensions, the physical and the conceptual. When someone enters the room, either the conceptual or physical part of the person is trapped inside, and the other is left to wander around free. I think the room was created by either a solely conceptual or solely physical person to isolate the other dimension so that either only the conceptual or the physical could inhabit the world? As for the hidden messages in the blackboxes, I guess that it screwed up and either a physical or conceptual guy got stuck in there when he shouldn't have, and they're trying to retrieve him.
I have no idea, and if an SCP can make me ask questions like this, it's probably good. +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
While agreeing with the general response, I still have to ask: What the actual bloody hell. +1

Edit: Tons of people hanging out in an impossibly small space, growing more and more agitated the longer they're stuck in there… Weird transmissions with no clear source trying to reassure them and explain the apparently-unfamiliar concept of waiting… Outsiders getting stuck in behavioral loops as they're unable to "update" their knowledge of certain things… A few people exploring the world outside the room and "killing" its inhabitants seemingly at random…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Love this

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Looked up waht a pataphor is and decided to upvote, still dont know what the CÉCE thing is, but the rest is cool

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: it was interesting, but, i just cant vote either way.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I can't complain about the use of black boxing here. In articles where I don't like it, it's usually out of place and frustrating, but here it adds a sense of danger to the missing information.
I also loved the idea of all these people being reassigned to the room. How do we know they're actually there?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I guess there's something else we're missing here…

+ SPOILER ALERT!
- Told ya

Look closely at the blackboxes
Oh, just select them.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This should be higher rated

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I always highlight ALL the blackboxes because of articles like this.
Regardless +1 good article

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Okay, so the basic thing seem pretty straightforward if you read it trough carefully. It's a room, if you step into it and go out people will remember you as being still in there, (I assume if you know of the effect but don't know the people affected very well you're not affected as much, because you'll be thinking less of the actual person and more of whatever mental image you have of the person, and it just wont match up enough to trigger the effect).

The implication being that you, or some fundamental part of you, is trapped in the room.

Weber being a foundation agent somehow managed to either make a deal or… break something and get out. Which makes sense, that's the stuff a foundation agent might manage. But he went crazy. What I'm wondering is what the SCP he "got ahold of" was.

Also, I found the entry at the wanderer's wiki with references to both CECE and Caliginator and I… really don't see the connection. Labradors? Wtf.

Anyway, I voted it up because the basic effect wes great, and the breakout and what happened afterwards was great to.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I don't think so? Since the observers are still capable of perceiving them leaving, they just think the affected are still inside despite the fact they left. So it's more like holding two contradicting beliefs simultaneously. And only the knowledge of the location of the affected is affected; since the description of the effect doesn't make any specific claims about the location of the affected, it wouldn't be affected. So the Foundation staffer that wrote about the skip knows that the people thinking about the affected individuals think they're still inside the room, and he also knows that all the affected individuals are still inside the room. He may deduce from that that his knowledge about the position of the affected individuals may be erroneous, but it doesn't change the fact that they're still all inside the room.

Argh, my brain.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Khym, you pick out logical consequences like nobody else…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Shouldn't the SCP affect whatever Foundation staffer wrote about the SCP? In which case the description would say something like "anyone who enters the room in unable to leave, but seem to be able to affect things at a distance"?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I felt pretty genre-savvy when I checked the negative space without anyone prompting me.
But the feeling faded because even with the extra info i can't put my finger on what's really going on here.

I like the base concept enough to up-vote. It makes me think of an intellectual dreamcatcher. The room acts like a filter on the "astral" plane, catching the concept of people but letting their reality slip thru. But I can't muster much enthusiasm about it because of this elephant of a back story in the room that I'm just not grokking.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The reason i missed this the first time is that I went "a-HA!" and:

zomg spolierz!!1
...

highlighted the first large blackboxed area, there was nothing there but dashes. This led me to believe that you had just chosen a more visually appealing way (no font-dependent 1-pixel lines between the characters) of blackboxing things out. As such, I didn't highlight any of the others the first time. I know it's supposed to be subtle, but it'd have been nice to have a tiny little bone thrown out in the first big blackboxed area to give the inveterate highlighter something to work with to know they're on the right track.


Other than that, excellent. Here, have my upvote to bring this to +40.

E: edited spoiler text as a tip of the hat to the article :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Aren't you still there?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+ show block
– hide block

SINCE WHEN IS THAT A THING?

And I'd originally tried it on the first one, found nothing but dashes, and figured that was the case for all of them. It wasn't the best idea.

Having read the entire thing now, I find myself incredibly confused about what's going on (I still say large parts of this are pretty dense), but I'm rescinding my downvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like this very much, but mostly because of the core concept and Experiment 1383-21. The whole Weber thing, and the hidden bits, I don't feel strongly one way or another.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The little messages were clever. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's something that I've introduced over at the Wanderer's Library wiki, and that may get more development here as well.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I posted this in the discusssion for Pulaski-Maraschino, so this is hardly new information.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Spoiler
– hide block

Try highlighting all of the blacked out parts

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: But… We don't do that. We have expungements and redactions solely so we don't have enormous black bars on articles.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It became pretty confusing towards the end, and I'm still not 100% clear on the CeCe concept. However it's certainly not boring and the thing with black boxes is quite clever. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is too long. Honestly, I would've been happy if you had ended it with the interview about Dr. Frey. You gave away too much and got too wordy, and by the end I was forcing myself to get through it.

This might just be a personal preference of mine, though, so don't change it unless a few other people feel the same way. Reluctant -1 from me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Oh, that was clever. Both of you, Scantron and author.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Previously no voted, but after reading those notes you've changed my mind.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Great SCP and tone, also love the little hidden messages in the huge black boxes +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thanks, fixed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That is the actual Motel 6 in Gilroy.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Erm. Was that an arbitrary hotel photo, or is that an actual motel room in Gilroy? I ask because, if the latter, there is a nonzero chance that I stayed in that motel >.>

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Can you assassinate a "has" in this sentence please?

"behavior as prompted by this incident has has yet…"

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The idea is to replicate the look you get on actual declassified documents, where swathes of text have been obscured with marker.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I am still really confused about what is going on here. It seems like going into the room separates your conceptual self with your physical self, and there is a conceptual world where your conceptual self can now act and do things separately. People think of this world and remember it, but don't perceive it. The Agent's conceptual self tried to break the globe thing and killed a bunch of people and was able to free itself by interacting with some other skip.

But this doesn't explain the blackboxes.

Or maybe the room really DOES trap people, and now their concepts are free to walk around, making people (and the foundation) think they're fine when they're not. This still doesn't explain the blackboxes.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's a stylistic thing. Maybe copy-paste what you're talking about here so we know we're on the same page? :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The initial description is a tad confusing. The part that makes it not confusing really seems like it ought to be in a test log. And why are there enormous swathes of blackboxes?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Somebody missed something.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's ok, but I didn't enjoy it enough to upvote. Also, those long black boxes are really annoying.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
…other sapient beings (see Addendum)…

Shouldn't this be "see Incident 1383-A"? That's the part where the item with intelligence roughly equivalent to a dolphin's is mentioned…

Also, there is a lot going on here. But I like it. I like the time period it's set in and the sense of a developing story that leads to some tentative conclusions but never really a single explanation. Several good examples of expungement as well.

e: typo

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm missing something big here. The idea is certainly interesting, but I'm missing the whole mystery behind it. I like the way you ued the blackboxes. At first i was annoyed, but then i checked and was pleased.

No Vote until i get a handle on this. I'm not sure if i'm just off my game or what, but I'm missing a lot of subtleties today…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Now this is one great piece of work. As the others said, I loved how subtle it was.

+1 from me, I look forward to more of your work.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
You've got an SCP-XXX in there, might want to fix it. *goes back to finishing reading*

EDIT: Only reason I didn't fix that myself is I'm not 100% positivie it's supposed to be SCP-1383, so figure it's best to leave alone.

Overall, my first impression was meh…and then I read it again, properly this time. Love it! Subtle and great, have another +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Does anyone else have the impression that this is a reverse 055?

+1.

E: Went back and reviewed the highlights again… now I wish I could upvote twice.

Chilling, man. Really chilling.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+1

Very inviting. I really want to learn more. How did you write this entire thing from a windowless hotel room anyway?

I liked how it started mundane-ish and then slowly built into something sinister. Great job.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It seems like I always post SCPs with an overlooked anachronism. Fixed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
An error, I believe: you say that it was discovered due to an online news story, but the Foundation apparently acquired control of it in the 70s.

As for the SCP itself… I usually like mindfuck concepts, and this one is entirely about conceptual reality versus conventional reality, but there's something… off about it that just isn't clicking for me. You obviously have a clear idea about what this is and what it's doing, but I'm missing something. No vote for now.
